name: JS & TS fundamentals (Lufthansa)
description: Spoken-interview versions of the Lufthansa quiz topics: equality and coercion, const semantics, promise chains, and TypeScript's type system.

topics.name: JavaScript language basics
topics.description: Equality, coercion, const semantics, template literals and other core language behavior.

topics.name: Promise chains & error handling
topics.description: How .then/.catch/.finally compose and where errors travel in a chain.

topics.name: TypeScript type system
topics.description: Structural typing, unions, generics, and what the compiler actually guarantees.

questions.title: == vs === and type coercion
questions.prompt: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript, and what surprises does type coercion cause?
questions.answerStructureHint: Strict vs loose equality, how coercion rules bite (empty string, null/undefined), and the practical rule.

expectedPoints.label: === compares without coercion
expectedPoints.description: Strict equality checks type and value; loose equality converts operands first.

expectedPoints.label: Names a coercion surprise
expectedPoints.description: Examples like '' == 0, null == undefined, '0' == false being true.

expectedPoints.label: Practical rule
expectedPoints.description: Default to ===; the only common legit == use is x == null to match both null and undefined.

followUps.prompt: What does 1 + '2' evaluate to, and why is + special among the operators?

questions.title: What const actually protects
questions.prompt: You declare an array with const and then push into it. Does it work — and what does const actually guarantee?
questions.answerStructureHint: Binding vs value immutability, why mutation still works, and how to get real immutability.

expectedPoints.label: const freezes the binding, not the value
expectedPoints.description: Reassignment is forbidden; the referenced object/array stays mutable.

expectedPoints.label: Real immutability options
expectedPoints.description: Object.freeze (shallow), immutable updates (spread), readonly types in TS.

followUps.prompt: Why does React state benefit from immutable updates rather than mutation?

questions.title: Promise chains and where .catch belongs
questions.prompt: In a promise chain, where should .catch go and what happens to errors thrown mid-chain?
questions.answerStructureHint: Errors skip to the nearest rejection handler; catch placement decides what it covers; finally and async/await equivalents.

expectedPoints.label: Errors propagate to the nearest catch
expectedPoints.description: A rejection skips the following .then handlers until a catch (or second then-argument) handles it.

expectedPoints.label: Catch placement defines coverage
expectedPoints.description: A catch at the end covers the whole chain; a mid-chain catch recovers and the chain continues fulfilled.
acceptedSignals: at the end of the chain

expectedPoints.label: async/await mapping
expectedPoints.description: try/catch/finally is the await-equivalent; forgetting await loses the error.

followUps.prompt: What is the difference between Promise.all and Promise.allSettled when one request fails?

questions.title: TypeScript's structural type system
questions.prompt: TypeScript is structurally typed. What does that mean in practice, and how do unions and generics fit in?
questions.answerStructureHint: Compatibility by shape not name, union narrowing, generics for reusable typing, and compile-time-only guarantees.

expectedPoints.label: Compatibility by shape
expectedPoints.description: Two types are compatible if their members line up — names don't matter (duck typing).

expectedPoints.label: Unions and generics
expectedPoints.description: Union types with narrowing (typeof/in/discriminated unions); generics parameterize reusable code.

expectedPoints.label: Types vanish at runtime
expectedPoints.description: No runtime enforcement — external data still needs validation (zod, guards).

followUps.prompt: An untyped npm package makes everything 'any'. How do you contain that?
